skill.name: waste-disposal-guide
skill.description: Guide for disposing specific chemical wastes into the correct colored waste containers, with safety precautions and regulatory compliance notes.
skill.summary: A reference guide for identifying the correct colored waste container for laboratory chemical disposal.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 29 static analysis findings were evaluated and determined to be false positives. The skill is a legitimate chemical waste disposal reference guide with no network calls, no external command execution, and no malicious intent. This skill is safe to publish.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptographic algorithm - false positive
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 13 locations as 'weak cryptographic algorithm' but all contain chemical/laboratory terminology (chloroform, mixture, compound) that the scanner misidentified as cryptographic terms. No cryptographic operations exist in this skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All flagged lines contain chemical names or metadata about chemical waste, not cryptographic algorithms. The scanner confused chemistry terminology with crypto terminology.

low_findings.title: Ruby/shell backtick execution - false positive
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 7 locations as 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' but all backtick instances are standard markdown code fence markers and inline code formatting in SKILL.md usage examples. No shell execution occurs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All backtick characters are part of markdown code block fences or inline code formatting in documentation. No Ruby code or shell execution exists in this skill.

low_findings.title: System reconnaissance - false positive
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 5 locations as 'system reconnaissance' but all contain chemical names in data dictionary entries and markdown table cells. No system reconnaissance occurs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Flagged lines contain chemical names like 'cadmium', 'arsenic', 'lead' in data definitions and markdown tables. These are legitimate waste category entries, not reconnaissance activity.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL - false positive
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged a hardcoded URL in SKILL.md, which is a legitimate source attribution link to the GitHub repository. This is standard open-source attribution and not a security concern.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is a standard GitHub repository source link used for attribution. No data exfiltration, credential transmission, or network call behavior exists in this skill.

content.user_title: Look Up Chemical Waste Disposal Containers and Safety Rules
content.value_statement: Lab workers often struggle to identify the correct waste container for chemical disposal. This skill provides instant guidance to the proper colored waste container along with safety precautions and regulatory notes for hazardous laboratory chemicals.
actual_capabilities: Looks up the correct colored waste container for a given laboratory chemical
Provides safety precautions and disposal instructions for each waste category
Handles mixed waste streams by identifying the most hazardous component
Lists all waste categories with their accepted chemical types and container colors
Reports unknown chemicals and recommends EHS verification
Documents mixture handling rules for halogenated solvent precedence
limitations: Does not cover radioactive, biological, or controlled substance waste disposal
Cannot provide emergency spill response guidance
Local regulations may differ from the general guidelines provided
Database covers common chemicals only and may not include specialized compounds

use_cases.title: Quick Chemical Container Lookup
use_cases.description: A lab technician needs to dispose of chloroform and uses the skill to find it belongs in the orange halogenated container with relevant safety notes.

use_cases.title: Mixed Waste Disposal Guidance
use_cases.description: A researcher with a mixture of chloroform and ethanol learns the halogenated component takes precedence and uses the orange container.

use_cases.title: Safety Training Reference
use_cases.description: An EHS officer uses the skill to train new lab staff on waste segregation by container color and chemical compatibility rules.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Chemical Lookup
prompt_templates.prompt: Look up the disposal container for chloroform.
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick lookup of a single chemical waste container

prompt_templates.title: Container Color Query
prompt_templates.prompt: What color container should I use for ethanol waste?
prompt_templates.scenario: Identify the container color for a specific chemical

prompt_templates.title: Mixed Waste Handling
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a mixture of chloroform and ethanol to dispose of. What container should I use?
prompt_templates.scenario: Determine correct container for a mixed waste stream

prompt_templates.title: Full Category Reference
prompt_templates.prompt: List all waste categories with their accepted chemicals and safety notes. Then explain how to handle mixed waste containing both halogenated and non-halogenated solvents.
prompt_templates.scenario: Comprehensive reference of all waste categories with mixture handling rules

output_examples.input: What container do I use for chloroform disposal?
output_examples.output:
Container: Orange (Halogenated Organic Waste)
Category: Halogenated
Safety Note: Keep separate from non-halogenated waste

Chloroform must go into the orange halogenated container. Do not mix with non-halogenated solvents in the same container.

output_examples.input: I need to dispose of a mix of chloroform and ethanol.
output_examples.output:
Chemical 1: Chloroform (halogenated - most hazardous)
Chemical 2: Ethanol (non-halogenated)

Container: Orange (Halogenated Organic Waste)
Rule Applied: Mixed waste containing halogenated solvents must go into the orange container regardless of other components.

Safety Note: Keep halogenated waste separate from non-halogenated waste streams.

output_examples.input: What are all the waste container categories?
output_examples.output:
1. Orange - Halogenated Organic (chloroform, DCM)
2. Red - Non-Halogenated Organic (ethanol, acetone)
3. Blue - Aqueous (buffers, saline)
4. Yellow - Acid (HCl, sulfuric acid)
5. White - Base (NaOH, KOH)
6. Gray - Heavy Metal (mercury, lead)
7. Black - Solid (gloves, paper)

Never mix acids with bases. Contact EHS for heavy metal disposal.

best_practices: Always verify the chemical name before selecting a waste container for disposal.
When handling mixed waste, identify the most hazardous component first to determine the correct container.
Consult your institution EHS office for local regulations that may differ from general guidelines.
anti_patterns: Using this skill for emergency spill response instead of contacting EHS immediately.
Assuming all institutions use the same color coding without local verification.
Disposing of unknown chemicals without proper identification and EHS guidance.

faq.question: What types of waste does this skill cover?
faq.answer: This skill covers standard laboratory chemical waste categories including halogenated, non-halogenated, aqueous, acid, base, heavy metal, and solid waste.

faq.question: Can this skill handle mixed waste streams?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill identifies the most hazardous component in the mixture and assigns the container for that component. Halogenated solvents take precedence over non-halogenated.

faq.question: Does this skill cover radioactive or biological waste?
faq.answer: No. This skill only covers standard laboratory chemical waste. It does not cover radioactive, biological, or controlled substance waste.

faq.question: What happens if the chemical is not in the database?
faq.answer: The skill reports the chemical was not found and suggests the closest category based on chemical class. It recommends contacting EHS for proper disposal guidance.

faq.question: Can I use this skill for emergency spill response?
faq.answer: No. This skill is not designed for emergency spill response. Contact your institution EHS office immediately for spill assistance.

faq.question: Are waste container colors the same at every institution?
faq.answer: No. Local regulations and institutional policies may differ. Always verify waste container color coding with your institution EHS office.
